👋🏼 Founders - it's time to add the 'AI Survival Slide' to your pitch decks. It's what every investor is thinking to themselves anyway so make sure to include some version of it. This week on a long flight without wifi, I reviewed 50+ startup decks that all included the standard slides: problem, solution, team, market etc. But as I flipped through them, I realized that I was waiting for a slide that never came. Right now, how a startup is going to survive AI (and thrive) is top of mind but it's still missing from most pitches. In 2026, the "Competitive Landscape" slide with the checkmarks is no longer enough. Investors don't just want to know how you're better than your current rivals, but why you won't be steamrolled by the next LLM update or a big tech giant adding your core feature to their suite. Don't get me wrong, many startups have good answers to this concern but are not specifically addressing it in their decks. This feels like a mistake in today's uncertain market. Especially if you are building in the b2b / enterprise space, your deck needs to address defensibility through a new lens that may include: 🎯 Deep workflows, not just widgets. Features are easy to replicate but deep, messy, and sticky integrations into a company’s daily operations are not. If your AI is a sidecar chatbot, you’re at risk. If it’s the engine that runs a complex internal process, you’re building a moat. 🎯 Proprietary data loops. Investors are looking for how you are capturing unique private data, the kind of specialized industry intel that isn't available for training public models. That data loop is your fortress. 🎯 The trust layer. In regulated industries like health, finance, or even legal, human-in-the-loop and brand can be a moat. AI can generate the work, but humans still need to sign off on it. How does your product own that trust? Most of what I listed above is already top of mind for the best founders but I'm not seeing it come thru in the actual pitch decks. Don't wait for the investor to ask....Why will you survive? Make it crystal clear and part of the pitch itself.

Today, we’re killing 2,000 integration companies. Including our own. After 12 years of work, we’re open-sourcing the world’s largest integrations knowledge base for AI agents: → connects any AI agent to 250+ apps → plugs it with 47,000+ actions → each API endpoint and auth flow is verified → battle-tested by 17,000 customers We’re making everything open source. Our former company, Pica, is now One. One lets you connect and monitor your AI agents from one place. So instead of stitching together brittle integrations, expired OAuth tokens, rate limits, broken permissions, and random API docs… your agents just work. Here’s what that looks like: → One CLI command to connect to hundreds of apps → precise permissions, so agents can’t go rogue → One Auth Link, so users can share app access without handing over API keys → One Flow, so your agent can run deterministic automations for almost no cost Knowledge should be free. Using it should be affordable. So comment "One" below and repost to get: → UNLIMITED connections → 1M API requests FREE every month → 47,000 integration actions

AI Changed How I Am Building My Next Roll Up 🔝 Two years ago, sourcing an acquisition target meant hiring a broker, paying $10-20K for a CIM, and spending weeks on manual research. Today I wake up at 4am and my AI stack does what used to take a team of 3: My problem is I now have too much deal flow. → Perplexity scans industries and pulls market data in minutes → Claude Dispatch runs my outreach and research workflows simultaneously → Claude Cowork builds the analysis docs, financial models, and diligence frameworks My Opex to get started on a deal went from five figures to a nominal cost. →The barrier to acquiring businesses has drastically changed. →You don't need a fund. →You don't need a team of analysts. →You need a framework, AI tools, and the discipline to wake up early and put in the work before anyone else is online. The people who figure this out in 2026 will build portfolios that took the last generation a decade to assemble. Subscribe to my newsletter on the latest ways to build a platform roll up today.
